input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is a 1/10‑scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensors and control technology. Its main hardware layers comprise a lower‑level chassis, an upper‑level chassis, and autonomy elements (e.g., perception and actuation modules) that together enable Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) experimentation in a constrained, safety‑critical environment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin delivers three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a user interface displays real‑time vehicle state, projected driving path, and detected object tracks; it also allows users to view, save, and generate reports of collected data. (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm is executed off‑board (e.g., in the cloud), where it determines the lead vehicle, computes a desired speed based on inter‑vehicle distance, and returns the speed command to the vehicle for actuation. (3) Remote Management – operators can remotely enable/disable ACC, set a desired speed, and issue an emergency braking command, with low‑latency bidirectional communication supporting safety‑critical operation.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The DT is built on NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 SDK for GPU‑accelerated processing, uses Robot Operating System 2 (ROS 2) as the middleware layer, and implements application modules in Python 3.8, C++, and MATLAB/Simulink for algorithm development and simulation. System data are persisted in an SQL database, and the cloud off‑loading environment provides the computational resources required for the ACC logic. The UI is integrated with these components to present real‑time visualizations and control interfaces.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires clock synchronization between devices with an accuracy of 100 ms, which is the maximum tolerance imposed by hardware limitations; this synchronisation frequency is used to enable safe computation off‑loading in the vehicular context.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin is organized into three top‑level entities—Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity—each deployed on separate hardware components. Within each entity, multiple Functional Entities (FEs) are instantiated (e.g., the User Interface FE is split into Visualization and Control Panel sub‑components, and a Data Storage component exists without a natural FE mapping). This results in a decentralized, hierarchical architecture where responsibility for sensing, actuation, data management and user interaction is distributed across distinct entities and their constituent FEs.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The overall orchestration follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture, mapping each Functional Entity to a ROS 2 package; each package contains one or more ROS 2 nodes that implement the entity’s functionality. Communication between the Physical Twin Entity (hosting compute, sensors, actuators and services such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking), the Digital Twin Entity (handling data pre‑processing, storage and control), and the User Entity (providing 3D visualization and control panels) is realised through ROS 2 publish/subscribe and service mechanisms, enabling component integration and resource management across the distributed hardware.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The DT incorporates several computational models and associated data pipelines. A point‑cloud clustering and tracking model processes raw LiDAR point clouds; clustering is performed with the DBSCAN algorithm and subsequent object tracking uses a Kalman filter. An Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) model determines the lead vehicle and computes a safe following speed. Data generated by these models (object tracks, ego‑vehicle state) are persisted in a relational SQL database via the Data Storage component. Model inputs originate from onboard sensors (LiDAR, depth camera, vehicle CAN bus) and the models are encapsulated in ROS2 nodes within the Digital Twin Entity.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is realized through a Vedder electronic speed controller that receives voltage commands derived from desired speed set‑points, and a lower‑level chassis controller that commands steering angle and vehicle speed. The Actuation software component translates high‑level speed and steering commands into motor voltage signals for the speed controller and steering actuator. Remote commands such as emergency braking are supported via ROS2 service calls, enabling the DT to issue real‑time control actions to the physical twin.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The architecture requires that all distributed components maintain a common notion of time with a synchronization accuracy of at most 100 ms, as dictated by hardware constraints and safety‑critical timing requirements. Consequently, the communication infrastructure must guarantee clock alignment within this bound, and any computation off‑loading between devices is designed to respect the 100 ms latency ceiling. Temporal fidelity is therefore a primary validation criterion for the twin’s operation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensors on the physical twin (LiDAR point‑cloud, vehicle state, motor status, steering servo status) are sampled and provided to the Data Monitoring pipeline. Raw LiDAR points are fed to the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking front‑end (FE) which clusters the points (e.g., using a DBSCAN‑type algorithm) to generate object tracks. All sensor streams are transmitted from the physical twin to the digital twin over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP at the transport layer. Inside the digital twin, software components communicate via ROS 2 middleware. The Data Translation FE serialises each stream, aligns message types and performs unit conversions, thereby integrating the heterogeneous data before it is consumed by the 3D Visualization, ACC computation and reporting services. The documents do not specify transmission frequency, event‑trigger conditions, or explicit data‑quality assurance mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Digital Twin computes Adaptive Cruise Control decisions (lead‑vehicle identification, desired speed) and sends control commands back to the vehicle over the same Wi‑Fi UDP link. Commands are packaged by the Data Translation FE into the vehicle’s actuation message format (e.g., desired speed set‑point, ACC enable/disable, emergency brake). The vehicle’s control unit receives the command, validates it, executes the corresponding actuation on motor and steering hardware, and provides implicit acknowledgment through subsequent state updates.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Intra‑digital‑twin communication between software components uses ROS 2 (DDS) middleware. Communication between the physical twin and the digital twin occurs over a Wi‑Fi network with UDP as the transport protocol. The topology is a logical client‑server exchange where the vehicle streams sensor data to the DT and receives control messages in return. The system is required to sustain low end‑to‑end latency for safety‑critical ACC operation, but the documents do not give quantitative latency, bandwidth, reliability, redundancy or security protocol details.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The ACC functionality is off‑loaded from the resource‑constrained ECU to the Digital Twin, which can be executed on cloud‑based compute resources or any platform capable of running ROS 2 services. The DT requires sufficient processing power for point‑cloud clustering, tracking, and ACC decision logic, as well as storage for collected vehicle state data and generated reports. The system is designed to maintain low end‑to‑end latency for remote management, though specific hardware, scalability mechanisms, and availability guarantees are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The development follows the ISO 23247 Reference Architecture: key software components are identified, then mapped onto Functional Entities (FEs). Each FE is realized as an individual ROS2 package that contains one or more ROS2 nodes delivering the required functionality. The mapping organizes the software according to the ISO‑based entity structure (User Entity, Digital Twin Entity, Physical Twin Entity) and each entity is deployed on a distinct hardware component. This entity‑based decomposition drives the engineering workflow, but the documents do not describe explicit quality‑assurance, testing, version‑control, or evolution‑management practices.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The User Entity provides a User Interface Functional Entity that is split into a 3D Visualization component and a Control Panel component, enabling operators to monitor the Digital Twin and issue control commands. The Physical Twin Entity hosts generic automotive functions such as point‑cloud clustering and tracking, which process raw sensor data into higher‑level perception information that can be visualized or used for control decisions. No specific analytics methods, confidence measures, or automated decision‑support algorithms are described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system is built on the ISO 23247 Digital Twin framework (parts 1–4), explicitly adopting its entity‑based reference architecture (Observable Manufacturing Element, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity). It acknowledges vehicular safety‑critical clock‑synchronization standards that mandate ≤100 ms accuracy, and mentions the possibility of using OPC Unified Architecture for device communication. The implementation uses ROS2 packages (leveraging DDS) to meet interoperability requirements.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the physical twin's scale, construction, hardware layers, and purpose. It is highly detailed and well-supported by the text. Quotes: 'The physical twin (PT) is a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle', 'physical architecture consists of three layers. The lower level chassis... The upper level chassis', 'developing an Adaptive Cruise Control DT'.

The extraction precisely describes the three core services (Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, Remote Management) with correct technical details about the UI, ACC algorithm logic, and remote commands found in the text. Quotes: 'develop the following three services through a DT', 'UI will display real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and detected object tracks', 'offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT', 'enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command'.

The extraction correctly identifies the software stack: NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0, ROS 2 middleware, Python 3.8, C++, MATLAB/Simulink, and an SQL database. However, it includes the phrase 'for GPU-accelerated processing' which describes the SDK's capability; while consistent with the NVIDIA hardware mentioned, this specific technical description is not explicitly stated in the source text provided.

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the 100 ms requirement for clock synchronization and its purpose (computation offloading), as well as the cause (hardware limitations). However, it incorrectly refers to this as a 'synchronisation frequency' whereas the source text specifies 'accuracy'. Supported by: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms', 'We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations', 'To enable computation offloading, devices must share the same notion of time'.

The description is highly accurate and detailed, correctly identifying the three top-level entities (Physical, Digital, User) deployed on separate hardware. It precisely captures the splitting of the User Interface FE and the unmapped status of the Data Storage component. Supported by: 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component', 'the User Interface FE has been split into two sub-components: visualization and control panel', 'one SW component, Data Storage did not have a naturally aligned FE'.

The extraction provides a strong summary of the ISO 23247 mapping and the specific responsibilities of each entity using ROS 2. However, it claims communication uses 'ROS 2 publish/subscribe and service mechanisms', which is not present in the provided source text; the text only mentions 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network'. Supported by: 'implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package', 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document.' The provided text focuses on the internal architecture and communication within the Digital Twin System (DTS). While the abstract mentions cloud-based fleet coordination as a general interest, the document does not provide specific details on integration with external systems, interoperability standards, or integration with other Digital Twins/enterprise systems.

_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)

The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item

processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the

_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement

provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,

in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin

automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the

component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly specific and thoroughly grounded in the source text. It accurately identifies the computational models: 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking' using 'DBSCAN' and 'Kalman filter' and the 'ACC' model which 'Determines lead vehicle and computes following speed'. It also correctly states that 'Data Storage' gathers data into a 'SQL database' and that components are implemented as 'ROS2 nodes' ('implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package').

The extraction correctly identifies the hardware and software components mentioned in the text, such as the 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller' and the 'lower level chassis' responsible for 'controlling the steering and the speed'. It accurately describes the 'Actuation' component's role in converting 'desired speed into motor voltage commands'. While the inference that remote commands use 'ROS2 service calls' is reasonable given the 'ROS2 network' mentioned, the specific service mechanism is not explicitly stated in the text, preventing a perfect score.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but Section III.B explicitly lists the onboard sensors: 'a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, an Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera, and a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller'. Table I and Figure 2 also reference 'LiDAR' and 'Vehicle State' as inputs. Since evidence exists in the source, the claim that it is missing is incorrect.

The extraction accurately synthesizes the timing and synchronization requirements found in Section IV.A. It correctly cites the '100 ms' synchronization accuracy (R8) and the '100 ms' latency ceiling (R7), correctly attributing these constraints to 'hardware limitations' and the need for 'computation offloading' in a safety-critical context.

end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_5_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture


1 [https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)
2 [https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

V. R EFLECTION AND C ONCLUSIONS


We reported on a case study of an automotive DT, through
which we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 standard
in engineering such systems. Our experiences highlight both
benefits and limitations of the standard in automotive settings.
On a positive note, we found the standard’s reference
architecture (RA) to be **a useful starting point for auto-**
**motive DTs** as it provides guidance for organizing blocks
of functionality. Additionally, we found the standard **easy to**
**use** as it does not assume substantial DT expertise and has a
comfortable learning curve. Our case was developed by two
graduate students, starting from a course project. [4] Through
this case study, we also highlight the **convenient generality**
of ISO 23247. The independence from specific format and
protocol requirements, as well as the ability to rely on domainspecific technologies in a straightforward manner, facilitated
the transfer of the RA to a new domain (automotive).
On a critical note, rigid adherence to the RA may lead to an
**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of
automotive DTs in more detail and elicit recommendations
